reason: Both answers correctly recommend a Postgres-backed queue, cite FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, transactional enqueue as the key advantage, point to real off-the-shelf libraries rather than hand-rolling, and give sensible thresholds for switching to Redis or SQS. They are nearly identical in structure and quality. Answer A gives slightly cleaner reasoning on when to revisit (vacuum pressure, queue availability independent of primary DB, easy migration later). Answer B adds a useful operational watch-out (pruning dead tuples/bloat, splitting the queue to its own instance if churn pressures the primary) but includes a slightly odd/imprecise library pick (Hatchet as a Python-specific option). These minor pluses and minuses roughly cancel out, leaving the two comparable in overall usefulness and correctness.
